title: Social Work Interventions in Juvenile Delinquency: Challenges and Prospects in Nigeria
abstract: In Nigeria, juvenile delinquency is still a major social issue that has a significant impact on neighborhood safety, social stability, and national advancement. By encouraging rehabilitation, reintegration, and preventive measures, social work interventions are essential in addressing the root causes of juvenile delinquent behavour. Numerous obstacles, such as insufficient institutional and policy frameworks, a lack of funds and resources, a lack of qualified social workers, and a lack of cooperation amongst juvenile justice system players, restrict the efficacy of these interventions. There are encouraging opportunities to advance social work practice in Nigeria in spite of these obstacles. Viable routes to better results include bolstering legal and policy frameworks, funding social worker capacity building, incorporating community-based strategies, and using technology for juvenile rehabilitation. This study looks at the difficulties social work interventions in juvenile delinquency face in Nigeria and considers the possibilities for developing a system that is more inclusive, durable, and responsive. The study adds to the conversation on improving the role of social work in fostering positive youth development and changing juvenile justice by pointing out both challenges and opportunities.
subject_area: SOCIAL WORK INTERVENTIONS IN JUVENILE DELINQUENCY

title: Post-Conflict Resettlement in North-Eastern Nigeria: Social Workers’ Role in Safeguarding and Psychosocial Support
abstract: This study investigates the role of social work training in mitigating resettlement challenges, enhancing safeguarding mechanisms, and promoting psychosocial resilience in post-conflict regions in Nigeria. Relying on extant literature, the study adopts a desk-based qualitative approach to synthesise findings from global and regional case studies, international development reports, and Nigerian scholarship. The analysis is framed by Galtung’s theory of peace and structural violence, which explains how displacement reproduces inequality; Coleman’s social capital theory, which highlights networks and trust as foundations of recovery; and Sen’s capability approach, which stresses the expansion of freedoms through access to education, healthcare, and security. These frameworks collectively guide the interpretation of resettlement as both a humanitarian and developmental process. Findings reveal that displaced populations face systemic deprivation, including inadequate infrastructure, weak institutional support, and exclusion from governance processes. Social work training in trauma-informed care, mediation, and participatory engagement emerges as a crucial factor in bridging humanitarian relief with long-term development, fostering sustainable reintegration. Safeguarding frameworks, particularly those protecting women and children, and psychosocial interventions, such as trauma healing and peace education, are indispensable for resilience-building. Hence, the study proposed a conceptual framework integrating “Risk assessment, training, safeguarding, psychosocial care, participation, monitoring, and institutionalisation collectively strengthen post-conflict resilience, protection, and sustainable community recovery.” The study recommends the professionalisation of social work, embedding safeguarding into national resettlement policies, strengthening psychosocial services, and promoting inclusive governance. By situating Nigeria within global post-conflict discourse, the research contributes to theory and practice, offering evidence-based strategies for holistic, community-driven recovery.

authors.name: Ogbanga Mina Margaret
authors.affiliation: Department of Social Work, Faculty of Social Sciences, Rivers State University, Port Harcourt

subject_area: Social Work and Sustainable Development Goals

title: Enhancing Social Work Response in Insurgency-Affected Areas: A Study of Mandatory Training and Certification in North Eastern Nigeria
abstract: The protracted Boko Haram insurgency in North Eastern Nigeria has generated a profound humanitarian crisis marked by mass displacement, trauma, and the erosion of social systems. Social workers occupy the frontline in mitigating these effects, yet many enter such volatile contexts without adequate preparation in trauma-informed, conflict-sensitive, and culturally nuanced practice. This study investigates the impact of a mandatory training and certification program on social work response in Borno, Adamawa, and Yobe states between 2021 and 2023. Employing a qualitative research design, data were gathered through in-depth interviews and focus group discussions with 30 trained social workers drawn from governmental and non-governmental organizations. The findings reveal that training significantly enhanced practitioners’ self-efficacy, equipping them with practical tools for trauma counseling, conflict mediation, and ethical decision-making in high-risk contexts. Participants emphasized cultural humility and community engagement as central to service acceptance, noting improved trust when interventions integrated local customs and Islamic principles. However, challenges remain, including limited institutional support, the risk of burnout, and the absence of continuous professional development structures. Critical components identified for sustainability include trauma-informed care, conflict resolution, and supervision frameworks. The study concludes that mandatory specialized training is not merely beneficial but ethically imperative for effective practice in insurgency-affected zones. It recommends institutionalizing certification, embedding training into national curricula, and strengthening systemic support to ensure sustainable, culturally responsive interventions. These findings provide evidence-based guidance for policymakers, professional bodies, and educators seeking to enhance social work capacity in conflict and post-conflict contexts.

authors.name: Ogbanga Mina Margaret
authors.affiliation: Department of Social Work, Faculty of Social Sciences, Rivers State University, Port Harcourt

title: Family Therapy Intervention on Adolescents with Substance Abuse Disorder in Yaba, Lagos State.
abstract:
Abstract
This study examined the effectiveness of family therapy interventions in reducing substance use disorders among adolescents in Lagos, Nigeria. A sample of 357 adolescents (ages 13-19) with diagnosed substance use disorders was recruited from five community health centres across Lagos State. Participants were randomly assigned to either the family therapy intervention group (n=178) or a treatment-as-usual control group (n=179). The family therapy intervention consisted of 16 weekly sessions spanning four months, combining structural family therapy and multidimensional family therapy approaches, selected for their cultural appropriateness and demonstrated efficacy in addressing family dynamics common in the Nigerian context. The intervention specifically targeted family communication patterns, parental monitoring, and the development of family-based coping strategies. Treatment fidelity was maintained through regular supervision and adherence checks. Outcome measures were assessed at baseline, post-intervention (4 months), and 6-month follow-up, using validated instruments. Results indicated that adolescents in the family therapy group showed significantly greater reductions in substance use frequency and severity and there is a significant prevalence of substance use among adolescents in Lagos State, Nigeria (p<0.001, d=0.82) compared to the control group at both post-intervention and follow-up assessments. Additionally, families dealing with adolescent substance use disorders in Lagos State demonstrate low awareness and relevance of family therapy as a treatment intervention (p<0.0, d=0.67). The four-month intervention duration was determined based on prior research suggesting optimal engagement while minimizing attrition in this population. Equally, the findings show that family therapy intervention significantly improves family communication patterns and reduces the frequency and severity of substance use among adolescents. These findings show that culturally adapted family therapy interventions can be effective in reducing substance use disorders among Nigerian adolescents, showing the importance of involving family systems in treatment approaches. Based on this, the study recommended that the Government should incorporate family therapy services into primary healthcare facilities, substance abuse treatment centres, and school counselling programmes to normalise this treatment approach and increase its accessibility.
	
Keywords: Family therapy, Interventions, Adolescents, Disorders, Substance Use, Yaba, Lagos State

authors.name: Sunday John Udoh
authors.affiliation: University of Lagos,, Master in Social Work

title: OIL RESOURCE CRISIS AND SOCIAL WORK INTERVENTION IN EMOHUA LOCAL GOVERNMENT AREA, RIVERS STATE, NIGERIA
abstract: The oil resource crisis in Nigeria’s Niger Delta, particularly in Emohua Local Government Area of Rivers State, reflects a paradox of wealth and deprivation. This study examines the impact of oil exploitation in Emohua and the role of social work interventions in addressing resulting challenges such as environmental degradation, unemployment, and poverty. Guided by Ecological Systems Theory, the study employed a descriptive qualitative design, utilizing secondary data analyzed through thematic content analysis. Findings revealed that oil activities have caused severe environmental damage including soil infertility, water pollution, and deforestation which have disrupted traditional farming and fishing livelihoods. Social work interventions such as health education, environmental rights advocacy, and psychosocial support were found to be vital in mitigating the adverse effects on community well-being. However, weak governance, lack of accountability, and poor policy implementation have intensified the crisis, forcing social workers to move beyond traditional roles into advocacy, community mobilization, and conflict resolution. These expanded roles highlight the importance of integrating social work practice into local governance and environmental policymaking in oil-producing regions. Recognizing and institutionalizing social workers within development frameworks will strengthen their capacity to promote justice, sustainable development, and community resilience in the Niger Delta.

authors.name: Emeodu, Elijah Nwabueze (Ph.D)
authors.affiliation: Ignatius Ajuru University of Education, Rivers State

title: Women’s Role in Promoting Sustainable Communities: A Literature-Based Perspective
abstract: This paper examines the pivotal role of women in promoting sustainable communities through a comprehensive review of existing literature. Women have historically been central to natural resource management, environmental activism, and the transmission of traditional knowledge systems, thereby contributing to ecological sustainability. Their engagement in community-based initiatives enhances social cohesion, equity, and intergenerational welfare, while their participation in governance and policy-making has been shown to prioritize essential services such as water, sanitation, and health. Despite these contributions, structural barriers such as unequal access to resources, exclusion from decision-making, and entrenched gender norms continue to restrict women’s potential in sustainability transitions. The article emphasizes the need to integrate gender-sensitive approaches into sustainability frameworks, recognizing women not merely as beneficiaries but as key agents of change. A gender-inclusive strategy is essential to achieving the Sustainable Development Goals and fostering resilient, equitable, and sustainable communities.

title: Sociocultural Factors Influencing Mental Health Rehabilitation in Ibadan, Oyo State
abstract: This study investigates the influence of sociocultural factors on mental health rehabilitation outcomes in Ibadan, Nigeria, focusing on the roles of negative cultural beliefs, family support, and social stigma. Using a descriptive survey design, data were collected from 41 medical professionals including doctors, nurses, psychologists, medical social workers, and occupational therapists at the State Hospital, Adeoyo. Regression analyses were employed to test the hypotheses. Findings revealed that negative sociocultural beliefs, such as supernatural attributions of mental illness, did not significantly predict rehabilitation outcomes (R² = .040, p = .192). Conversely, family support showed a statistically significant positive influence on recovery outcomes (R² = .080, p = .045), highlighting the critical role of familial involvement in mental health recovery. Social stigma, though prevalent, was not found to significantly predict delayed or discontinued rehabilitation participation (R² = .032, p = .287). These results suggest that while traditional beliefs and stigma remain part of the sociocultural landscape, they may be moderated by factors such as education, exposure to modern care, and institutional support. The study recommends incorporating family-based interventions into mental health rehabilitation programs and calls for further research into additional mediating variables. A culturally grounded, family-centred approach is essential for improving rehabilitation outcomes in Nigeria.
subject_area: Mental Health, Psychiatric Rehabilitation, Psychiatric Social Work, Medical Sociology, Clinical and Community Psychology, Public Health, Health Policy and Management, Social Epidemiology, Cultural Anthropology and Ethnography of Health, Health Services Research, Health Education and Health Communication, Community Medicine or Primary Health Care, Psychiatric Nursing, Community Health, Occupational therapy and Psychosocial Rehabilitation, Disability Studies

title: Impact of Globalization on Indigenous Values and the Potential for Dialogue that Respect individual Autonomy and Collective Cultural Heritage in Nigeria
abstract:
ABSTRACT
The study examined the impact of globalization on indigenous values and the potential for dialogue that respects individual autonomy and collective cultural heritage in Nigeria. The impact of globalization on Nigeria's cultural heritage is complex and multifaceted. On the one hand, globalization has enabled the dissemination of Nigerian cultural expressions and customs to international arenas. Two research questions were formulated in the study. The study is a qualitative and quantitative survey using an ex-post-facto design. The sample used for this study was 150 participants in the study area. A simple random sampling technique was used in the study. A self-developed instrument titled “Impact of Globalization on Indigenous Values and Cultural Heritage Questionnaire - IGIVCHQ” and interviews were used as the instrument to collect data from participants. The researcher administered a total of 150 questionnaires to randomly selected participants and 15 participants were interviewed. Simple percentages, Mean scores, standard deviation, and Thematic statistics were used to analyse the data. The findings revealed that globalization has both positive and negative impacts on indigenous values and cultural heritage in Nigeria. In conclusion, globalization is rapidly shaping the cultural practices of the Nigerian people. Those who fail to adapt quickly are being left behind, and it appears that everyone is in a hurry to keep up. The cultural practices of developed economies have largely supplanted local culture, with many Nigerians preferring the cultural norms of these countries. This era has brought about a dual impact. While it has opened doors for international recognition and celebration of Nigerian culture, it has also posed substantial threats to the preservation of traditional values and practices. There is the crave for a more global than indigenous cultural identity that ought to be promoted.
funding_info: Author is funding from his pocket

title: An Examination of the Prevalence Predisposing Factors and Management of Malaria in Zamfara State
abstract:
ABSTRACT
This study was carried out on an examination prevalence of predisposing factors and management of malaria in Zamfara state .To achieve this objective, the researcher developed and administered a questionnaire on one hundred and twenty selected staffs of different agencies in Zamfara state. The Chi-square was used in testing the null hypothesis. From the study, It was observed that Malaria is common in their community. It was also observed that Most people have had malaria at least once in the past year. It was discovered that Malaria cases are not reported frequently in health facilities. It was also discovered that Poor sanitation in their community contributes to the prevalence of malaria. It was observed that the presence of stagnant water in their area increases the chances of malaria. The findings show that High humidity and climate conditions in my region make it conducive for malaria mosquitoes to breed. The finding also shows that Limited use of insecticide-treated bed nets increases the risk of malaria. finding also shows that they received education on how to prevent malaria. It was also discovered there is no adequate access to malaria treatment in their community. it was also discovered that Malaria management services in local health centers are not effective. Findings show that the government does not provide enough resources for malaria prevention and management. Findings also show that they have not received prompt medical treatment whenever they contract malaria. findings shows that Traditional medicines are commonly used in my community to treat malaria. Findings show that malaria can be completely eradicated in my area with proper management. The study recommends, Establish more healthcare centers in rural areas of Zamfara State to enhance access to malaria treatment and preventive care, especially for vulnerable populations. The state government should collaborate with stakeholders to implement sustained vector control programs, including indoor residual spraying and environmental management to reduce mosquito breeding sites. Provision of free or affordable malaria treatment, especially for pregnant women and children under five, to reduce mortality and morbidity rates. Local communities should be actively involved in malaria prevention initiatives, with trained health volunteers supporting surveillance, treatment, and education efforts. Invest in clean water supply and proper waste disposal systems to minimize conditions that promote mosquito breeding in communities. Expand the implementation of SMC programs, especially during peak malaria transmission seasons, to protect children and high-risk groups. Continuous monitoring of malaria prevalence, drug resistance, and effectiveness of control measures should be conducted to adapt policies and interventions accordingly.

title: Quantitative Analysis of Enrollees’ Satisfaction with National Health Insurance Scheme at Ring-Road State Hospital, Adeoyo, Ibadan
abstract: This study investigates quantitative analysis of enrollees’ satisfaction with National Health Insurance Scheme at Ring-Road State Hospital, Adeoyo, Ibadan. Addressing a gap in localized studies, the research is anchored on expectancy disconfirmation theory and economic theory theories to investigate enrollees’ satisfaction. A mixed-methods design is adopted, combining quantitative and qualitative approaches. The population comprises 796 principal NHIS enrollees at Ring-Road State Hospital, Adeoyo, Ibadan. Using Slovin formula, 266 participants are selected through purposive sampling technique for the survey. Structured questionnaires are used to collect data. Accessibility is measured with Perceived Access to Healthcare Questionnaire (PAHQ)-α=0.86, Affordability is measured with Perceived Affordability Scale (PAS)-α=0.77, and enrollee satisfaction is measured with Patient Satisfaction Questionnaire (PSQ-18)- average α=0.72. Quantitative data are analyzed with descriptive and inferential statistics (including correlation and regression analysis). Results shows significant joint influence to the prediction of enrollees’ satisfaction by accessibility and affordability (F (2,263) = 129, p < .01; R2 = 0.49), with accessibility having the stronger significant independent influence to the prediction (β = 1.06, t = 14.76, p < .01). The study concludes that accessibility and affordability are strong determinants of enrollees’ satisfaction. It recommends prompt and quality medical services, as well as reduction in out-of-pocket expenses during NHIS service consumption to improve enrollees’ satisfaction in state hospitals.
funding_info: The authors received no financial support for the research, authorship, and/or publication of this article.
conflicts_of_interest: The authors declared no potential conflict of interests with respect to the research, authorship, and/or publication of this article.

title: The Social Dynamics of Ageing and Coping Mechanisms of the Older Adult Abuse and Response to Abuse in Etinan Local Government Area, Akwa-Ibom State of Nigeria
abstract:
ABSTRACT
The paper investigates the prevalence of older adult abuse and social work intervention in Etinan Local Government Area, Akwa-Ibom State of Nigeria. The specific objectives were to ascertain the prevalence of different forms of abuse among older adults; assess the awareness and perceptions of abuse among older adults; identify the factors contributing to the vulnerability abuse among older adults; examine the impact of abuse on older adults; and evaluate existing Social Work Interventions among older adults in Nigeria. This paper discusses the coping mechanisms elderly abuse and neglect among older adults and the effectiveness of social interventions in Etinan Local Government Area, Akwa Ibom State of Nigeria. The findings, however, show that older adults are still facing a myriad of challenges owing to a significant number of abuses and neglect being perpetuated by their families, caregivers, and society at large and it also indicates a significant association between educational level and perception of social work intervention services for older adults. The following recommendations were made: that there should be training programmes for social workers focusing on recognizing, reporting, and responding to elder abuse and that local radio and television stations should sponsor the dissemination of information about elder rights and available support services; and also punishment should be meted out on those who violate the rights of the elderly adults in Nigeria.

title: Appraisal of Social worker and legal practitioners’ engagement: The collaborative aggregate for social justice in Nigeria
abstract: The study was on appraisal of social worker and legal practitioners’ engagement: The collaborative aggregate for social justice in Nigeria. Over the years there has been a strained relationship existing between and amongst that has limit the positive collaborative engagement between social workers and legal practitioners. In Nigeria this two independent professions never knew of any of their complementary role. This has been more conspicuously conspicuous in social workers and legal practitioners’ routine engagement. Arising from ofngagementitioners'never knew of any complementary roleetween and amongst very.this stand point that this examined the challenges and the need for effective collaborative engagement and complementary role between social workers and legal practitioners in Nigeria directed towards optimal service delivery to not only their clients but the Nigerian society at large. In line with the issues under investigation, this paper adopted the social identity theory formulated by Henri Tajfel in 1970. This is one of the major theories on inter-relationship. The social identity theory is predicated on the assumption that an individual develops his/her self-concept on the basis of his/her attachment to a significant group. Data usage were from both primary and secondary sources. Correspondingly, we employed key participant interviews (KPI) as instruments for data collection for this study, using ten (10) sample respondents each of social workers from University of Port Harcourt Teaching Hospital and legal practitioners in Rivers State High Court respectively as our target participants for experience generalization. The study’s findings informed that the legal practitioners and social workers are interwoven and has similarities in certain areas of their profession. It is on this note that the study recommended amongst others that Emphasis should be placed on lack of awareness, the non-professional nature of social work in Nigeria, among others such as differences in ethics, professional training and education.
subject_area: Appraisal of Social worker and legal practitioners’ engagement: The collaborative aggregate for social justice in Nigeria

title: Effectiveness of Social Casework Methods in the Correction of Juvenile Delinquents in Selected Correctional Centres in Lagos State
abstract: The correction of juvenile delinquents while they are in correctional institutions is crucial to their effective reintegration into the community and their contribution to the development of society. This study examined the effectiveness of social casework methods in correcting juvenile delinquents in selected correctional centres in Lagos State. Using psychosocial theory, problem-solving theory, and social systems theory, this study employed a qualitative research approach, and content analysis was used for data analysis. Data were gathered from 35 juveniles and 12 social workers from Lagos State. Findings highlight the role of interviews in the gathering of information from the juveniles, which leads to the diagnosis of their problems and eventual individualised treatment. It also revealed that past trauma affects the juveniles’ ability to open up and engage positively in interviews, accurate social diagnosis is often hindered by the lack of training and the expertise of the correctional staff and effective treatment is often compromised due to inadequate funding, leaving juveniles without access to the support and resources needed for their total correction and successful reintegration into the community. The study recommends continuous training and professional development for social workers to enhance their effectiveness in juvenile corrections, adequate funding of the correctional centres by the State government and the engagement of trauma informed specialist in the interview processes at the correctional centres.
