title: Divorce and Child Upbringing: A Case Study of Bonny Local Government Area of Rivers State, Nigeria
abstract: This study investigated the effects of divorce on child upbringing in Bonny Local Government Area of Rivers State, Nigeria, with particular focus on emotional, academic, and social outcomes. Anchored on Bowlby’s Attachment Theory, the research emphasized that disruptions in caregiver-child bonds due to divorce negatively influenced children’s psychological development, peer relationships, and academic performance. A descriptive survey research design was employed, targeting 309,200 residents across five communities. A sample of 400 respondents was selected using purposive, quota, and convenience sampling techniques. Data were collected through structured questionnaires and analyzed using descriptive and inferential statistical methods, including weighted mean scores and contingency tables. Findings revealed that children from divorced families experienced significant emotional challenges, including anxiety, low self-esteem, and social withdrawal, which impaired their social interactions and peer relationships. Academic performance was also adversely affected, with children displaying poor concentration, absenteeism, and reduced motivation due to disrupted home environments and inconsistent parental support. Additionally, divorce influenced children’s social adjustment, with many struggling to establish and maintain healthy friendships. The study highlighted the critical role of social work services in providing counseling, peer support, and family interventions, although accessibility and service delivery limitations were noted. Recommendations emphasized enhancing social worker resources, implementing targeted counseling programs, encouraging parental involvement, and raising public awareness about the impacts of divorce on children. Overall, the study underscored the need for holistic interventions to mitigate the negative effects of divorce and promote children’s emotional, academic, and social well-being.

title: Social Works and Community Development in Essien Udim Local Government Area of Akwa Ibom State
abstract: Historically, Community development in most part of the world particularly Africa and in Nigeria has been sluggishly pursued. To enjoy modern amenities of society, and to have a vibrant economic status, Social workers have an important role in helping people to improve their community life style. Thus, this study seeks to investigate the relationship between social work and community development in Essien Udim Local Government Area of Akwa Ibom State. To achieve this objective, Community Development theory was used to anchor the study. The study used a survey design, with 246,600 population of Essien Udim, LGA of Akwa Ibom State (2025 estimated). The sample size was four hundred (400), which was determined using the Taro Yamen formula's with 95% confidence interval. The main research instrument used in collecting data for this study was a well-designed questionnaire whose responses provided the necessary data for validating the research hypothesis. The Data for this research were collected from two sources: primary and secondary sources. The reliability coefficient of the items created to assess the relationship between social work and community development was 0.87. The method of data analysis was the Karl Pearson Product-Moment Correlation Coefficient. The findings revealed that there exists a significant relationship between social work and community development in Essien Udim Local Government Area of Akwa Ibom State. Based on the findings, the study recommended, among others, that Essien Udim Local Government Area should increase the number of professional social workers in various communities to advise and give support to leadership and development issues. Also, Essien Udim Local Government Area should increase social work awareness at the community level. More so, the benefits opened to individuals and members of associations both locally and internationally be highlighted.

title: Test
abstract: Test

title: Artificial Intelligence and Human Interaction from the Nigerian Perspective. The role of Social Work in Digital Transformation
abstract: The largest economy in Africa and the most populous country, Nigeria is experiencing its own challenges in the integration of artificial intelligence (AI) technologies into the social work practice. This paper discusses the integration of AI and human interaction into the Nigerian social work system, including the opportunities and associated challenges related to the socio-economic, cultural, and technological realities in Nigeria. This paper explores the potential of AI to supplement existing social work practices and enhance service delivery. By examining existing literature, ongoing trends and developments, as well as situational factors. This paper argues that even though AI holds substantial promise in overcoming the social service delivery challenges faced by Nigeria, including poor infrastructure, scarce resources, and local demand, the introduction of AI solutions should be culturally sensitive and culturally suitable. The practice of social work in Nigeria is based on the community models and infusion of AI should not compromise these models. The results indicate that Nigerian social workers should develop technological capacity and champion inclusive AI development that addresses the digital divide, language diversity, and solutions that are culturally sensitive. Furthermore, this paper also highlights the need to implement the Ubuntu philosophy which places special emphasis on communal values, empathy, and mutual respect, in order to make sure that the AI-based interventions are properly in line with the social welfare objectives of the country.

title: Different Parental Support Systems and Truancy Intervention Effectiveness in Public Secondary Schools in Lagos State, Nigeria.
abstract:
Truancy remains a persistent educational and social issue with far-reaching implications for students’ development. Studies found that some parents abdicate their duty to the school by failing to keep an eye on their wards' progress and are not involved in the intervention process, which exacerbates the truancy problem. This study investigated the role of different parental support systems on truancy intervention effectiveness in public secondary schools in Surulere Local Government Area (LGA), Lagos State. The study adopted a quantitative research method. Data were collected from a sample size of 180 respondents using simple random sampling technique. The study location was Surulere LGA, Lagos State, covering six public secondary schools. The research instrument for collecting the quantitative data was a structured questionnaire on a face-to-face basis. Collected data was analyzed with SPSS version 23, and the results were presented using percentages, tables, frequency distribution, and cross-tabulations. The findings indicated that many parents do not attend PTA meetings/workshops to encourage their child's school attendance; neither do they attend counseling/support services related to truancy intervention programs. The findings of this study have implications for truancy intervention effectiveness by way of strengthening parental support systems through active engagement, communication, and collaboration with the school.
Key words: Effectiveness, Intervention, Parental Support Systems, Public Schools, Truancy.
keywords: Effectiveness, Intervention, Parental Support Systems, Public Schools, Truancy.

title: CLIMATE CHANGE AND STUDENT MENTAL HEALTH SUPPORT: A SYSTEMATIC REVIEW OF COUNSELLING SERVICES, ECO-THERAPY PROGRAMMES, AND CLIMATE-RESPONSIVE MENTAL HEALTH INTERVENTIONS AND THEIR EFFECTS ON ANXIETY AND DEPRESSION OUTCOMES AMONG STUDENTS AT THE UNIVERSITY OF LAGOS, NIGERIA
abstract:
University students globally are experiencing worsening mental health outcomes, with anxiety, depression, and emerging climate-related psychological conditions—eco-anxiety, climate grief, and ecological dissociation—forming a growing clinical concern. The University of Lagos (UNILAG), located within a highly climate-vulnerable urban environment in sub-Saharan Africa, serves approximately 60,000 students exposed to escalating flooding, urban heat stress, and sea-level rise. This study examines the adequacy of climate-responsive mental health support services for this population.
The study synthesises four theoretical frameworks: Clayton’s (2020) model of climate anxiety psychopathology; Lazarus and Folkman’s (1984) transactional stress and coping theory, adapted to Nigerian university contexts by Okafor and Nweke (2019); Antonovsky’s (1979, 1987) salutogenic sense of coherence framework; and Ulrich’s (1984, 1993) psychophysiological stress recovery theory. These frameworks are used to critically assess UNILAG’s counselling services, eco-therapy initiatives, psychoeducational group interventions, and campus-based environmental engagement programmes.
UNILAG’s Student Counselling and Health Services Division provides individual counselling primarily targeting generalised anxiety and depression, typically assessed using standardised tools such as the GAD-7, PHQ-9, and Beck Depression Inventory. However, climate-related psychological distress remains both diagnostically under-theorised and institutionally under-addressed. While eco-therapy and nature-based interventions demonstrate clear psychophysiological benefits for stress reduction, their implementation within campus mental health services remains fragmented and non-systematised. Similarly, peer-led psychoeducational programmes addressing climate anxiety and grief show potential for fostering shared meaning-making and psychological agency, but lack formal evaluation and institutional integration. The intersection of academic pressure, financial strain, digital social fragmentation, and direct environmental exposure produces a compounded transactional stress burden that conventional counselling models do not adequately capture or address.
Mental health services should incorporate climate-specific screening tools and intervention protocols that distinguish adaptive from maladaptive climate anxiety responses. Eco-therapy should be institutionalised as a core clinical intervention rather than an optional wellness activity, leveraging Lagos’s lagoon and green ecosystems. Peer-based climate grief support groups should be formalised and subjected to longitudinal evaluation. In addition, campus environmental stewardship programmes should be designed to simultaneously promote ecological engagement and psychological restoration of agency. Funding structures must prioritise climate-responsive mental health infrastructure alongside existing counselling provisions.
UNILAG students experience a concentrated climate–mental health burden shaped by Lagos’s acute environmental vulnerability. Addressing this requires a shift from predominantly pathogenic models of mental health care toward a salutogenic framework that enhances comprehensibility, manageability, and meaningfulness as core dimensions of resilience. Without such a shift, university mental health services risk remaining misaligned with the lived realities of climate-distressed student populations in Nigeria.

title: SUSTAINABLE WASTE MANAGEMENT PRACTICES AND STUDENT ENGAGEMENT: A SYSTEMATIC REVIEW OF INSTITUTIONAL RECYCLING, COMPOSTING INITIATIVES, AND PARTICIPATORY OUTCOMES AT THE UNIVERSITY OF PORT HARCOURT, NIGERIA
abstract:
A critical systematic review investigates whether sustainable waste management initiatives can enhance stu-
dent engagement in Nigerian universities, with particular reference to the University of Port Harcourt (Uni-
Port). The study synthesises literature on recycling schemes, composting systems, campus waste audits, and

community-based environmental stewardship programmes within higher education environments. It is the-
oretically anchored in Astin’s Theory of Student Involvement, Ajzen’s Theory of Planned Behaviour, the

Sustainable Development Framework, and the Community of Inquiry Model, which collectively explain how
participation, attitudes, perceived control, and social learning processes shape student behavioural outcomes.

Across international studies, there is generally consistent evidence that participatory environmental man-
agement programmes can improve student engagement by increasing involvement in institutional activities,

strengthening environmental awareness, and fostering a stronger sense of campus belonging. However, these

positive associations are highly dependent on programme design features such as student inclusion in de-
cision-making, curriculum integration, institutional support, and sustained resource allocation. Where such

conditions are absent, engagement outcomes tend to be weak or short-lived.
In contrast, the Nigerian higher education context presents significant structural and operational constraints,
including inadequate waste management infrastructure, limited environmental education integration, weak
institutional enforcement mechanisms, and underdeveloped student sustainability cultures. Within this setting,
the evidence base is notably sparse and largely non-empirical, relying heavily on conceptual discussions and

studies from dissimilar contexts. As a result, causal claims regarding the impact of sustainable waste manage-
ment on student engagement in Nigerian universities remain tentative.

The review therefore concludes that while sustainable waste management initiatives hold strong theoretical
promise as instruments for enhancing student engagement, the current strength of evidence is moderate and

primarily inferential. The findings highlight a clear need for context-specific, methodologically rigorous em-
pirical studies in institutions such as UniPort to establish more definitive relationships and to inform policy

and practice in Nigerian higher education sustainability governance.

title: Substance use among youths: A social work perspective on rehabilitation and support
abstract: This study examined the prevalence, psychosocial impacts, and rehabilitation outcomes of psychoactive substance use among youths in Rivers State, Nigeria with a view of the role of social work. A quantitative descriptive research design was employed, using a programmatic dataset representing 312 youths sampled across Port Harcourt City and Obio/Akpor LGAs. Data were collected on a 4-point Likert scale and analyzed with descriptive statistics, independent-samples t-tests, and paired-samples t-tests, with benchmarks for prevalence, intervention success, and relapse protection. Findings revealed a high prevalence of psychoactive substance use among youths (M = 3.15), consistent with national patterns of increasing drug experimentation. Substance users reported significantly lower psychosocial wellbeing than non-users, underscoring the psychosocial costs of drug use. Social work interventions counselling, family therapy, and community programs proved highly effective in reducing substance use, with post-intervention scores showing meaningful declines. Furthermore, family support and school engagement emerged as protective factors against relapse, with strong ties significantly lowering recurrence rates compared to weak support. The study concludes that youth substance use is not merely an individual health problem but a systemic social challenge shaped by family, school, and community contexts. Effective rehabilitation therefore requires holistic, multi-level interventions integrating counselling, family engagement, and community participation. Recommendations include; scaling up community-based prevention programs; integrating psychosocial counselling into rehabilitation; expanding evidence-based social work interventions such as CBT and SBIRT across schools and health centers; and institutionalizing structured aftercare programs that involve families, schools, and peer-support networks to ensure sustainable recovery.

title: TEACHERS’ IMPRESSION MISMANAGEMENT, CONCEALMENT OF ACCOMPLISHMENT THROUGH HUMBLE BRAGGING AND STUDENTS’ SELF-PROMOTION IN PUBLIC SECONDARY SCHOOLS IN DELTA STATE
abstract:
This study investigated teachers’ impression mismanagement, the concealment of accomplishments through humble bragging, and students’ self-promotion in public secondary schools in Delta State. Impression management, a central concept in social psychology, refers to conscious or subconscious efforts to influence others’ perceptions, often balancing the goals of appearing competent and likable. This investigation was guided by two research questions and two null hypotheses. A correlational survey with an ex-post facto design was
utilized.
The study population comprised all public secondary school principals and teachers in Delta State, totaling 479 principals and 14,877 teachers. A multi-stage sampling procedure produced a sample of 500 participants, comprising 100 principals and 400 teachers. Data were collected using the Teachers’ Impression Mismanage-
ment and Students’ Self-Promotion Questionnaire (TIMSSPQ). Reliability was established using Cronbach’s alpha, resulting in a coefficient (α) of 0.89. Responses were measured on a four-point Likert scale: Strongly Agree (SA), Agree (A), Disagree (D), and Strongly Disagree (SD). A total of 500 questionnaires were administered to randomly selected participants. Data analysis involved simple percentages, mean scores, standard deviation, coefficient of determination (with 2.50 as a benchmark), and Pearson Product-Moment Correlation
Analysis.
The findings indicated that teachers’ impression mismanagement and humble bragging affect students’ self-promotion. In a bid to appear modest, teachers downplay their achievements instead of emphasizing them.
In conclusion, these teacher behaviors impact students’ self-promotion through learning. Students are given the impression that it is wrong to brag about genuine accomplishment. In conclusion, modelling authentic
behaviours, promoting self-awareness, and encouraging genuine self-promotion can foster a positive learning environment that enhances students’ self-confidence, self-awareness, and resilience.

authors.name: URIEN James
authors.affiliation: Department of Psychology, Delta State University, Abraka, Nigeria

title: INCLUSIVE DISASTER RISK REDUCTION AND STUDENT SAFETY: A SYSTEMATIC REVIEW OF EMERGENCY PLANNING, COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT, DISABILITY-INCLUSIVE PREPAREDNESS, AND RESILIENCE-BUILDING STRATEGIES AND THEIR EFFECTS ON STUDENT VULNERABILITY AND SAFETY OUTCOMES AT THE UNIVERSITY OF LAGOS
abstract:
Student safety in Nigerian federal universities represents a multidimensional challenge encompassing physi-
cal protection, psychological security, emergency responsiveness, and institutional resilience. The University of Lagos, due to its coastal exposure, high population density, and proximity to industrial and urban hazards, presents a particularly complex disaster risk environment. Despite this, campus disaster preparedness and inclusive emergency planning remain limited, especially for vulnerable student populations such as persons with disabilities and socioeconomically disadvantaged groups.
This study systematically reviews existing literature on inclusive disaster risk reduction (DRR) strategies in higher education, with a focus on their applicability to the University of Lagos context. A protocol-driv-
en systematic review was conducted using multiple academic databases and grey literature sources. Studies were screened using predefined inclusion criteria, with quality appraisal conducted using the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool (MMAT). The synthesis integrates evidence across four theoretical perspectives: the Pressure
and Release Model, Social Vulnerability and Community Resilience theory, political ecology of disaster, and inclusive disaster risk reduction frameworks.

Findings indicate that inclusive DRR strategies—such as participatory emergency planning, disability-inclusive preparedness systems, community-based risk communication, and multi-channel early warning systems—consistently improve evacuation efficiency, preparedness awareness, and institutional resilience outcomes. However, implementation gaps persist in Nigerian universities due to weak governance structures, limited funding, and inadequate integration of inclusive design principles.
The study concludes that strengthening inclusive disaster risk governance at the University of Lagos requires institutionalising accessibility-focused emergency systems, enhancing community engagement in preparedness, and embedding DRR into university policy frameworks.

authors.name: Stella Karibo Ortuka
authors.affiliation: Department of Social Work Faculty of Social Sciences Rivers State University, Port Harcourt, Nigeria

authors.name: Mina Margaret Ogbanga
authors.affiliation: Department of Social Work Faculty of Social Sciences Rivers State University, Port Harcourt, Nigeria

title: ÈKÓ NKẸ́KỌ́ AND INCLUSIVE EDUCATION IN LAGOS: LEVERAGING SDG 4 TO ADDRESS EDUCATIONAL INEQUALITIES AND SOCIAL VULNERABILITIES AMONG ADULTS IN VULNERABLE URBAN COMMUNITIES
abstract:
The implementation of Èkó Nkẹ́
kọ́
(Lagos is Learning) as a strategic initiative that emphasizes inclusive and

equitable quality education is the focus of this research. This paper analyzes how the program addresses ed-
ucational inequalities, especially among adults from vulnerable urban communities such as those affected by

marginalization, labor difficulties and poverty in Lagos, Nigeria. By integrating the principles of social work,
Eko Nkẹ́
kọ́
provides not only access to education but also psychosocial support, counseling and advocacy
for adults facing barriers to learning. This article explores the social welfare outcomes of facilitating learner
engagement, promoting retention, and strengthening community partnerships, thereby mitigating social risks
that undermine educational outcomes. Drawing on empirical data, policy analysis, and best practice models,
the study identifies successes and challenges in operationalizing inclusive education in Lagos. The findings
underscore that initiatives like Èkó Nkẹ́
kọ́, can serve as a model for addressing systemic educational inequali-
ties, enhancing learner well-being, and advancing social justice. The article concludes by recommending poli-
cy enhancements, capacity building for educators, social workers, and community-driven strategies to sustain

inclusive education and achieve SDG 4 in urban Nigerian contexts.

title: IMPACT OF CHILD LABOUR ON HAWKING CHILDREN AND THE ROLE OF SOCIAL WORK INTERVENTIONS IN OVIA NORTH EAST LOCAL GOVERNMENT AREA, EDO STATE, NIGERIA
abstract:
Child labour remains a critical social issue in Nigeria, with street hawking being a common form of economic exploitation among children. This study examined the effects of child labour on hawking children and
assessed the role of social worker interventions in mitigating these risks, focusing on Ovia North East Local Government Area of Edo State. The study employed a descriptive survey design, involving 150 respondents
comprising hawking children for questionnaires administration and 10 participants which were parents or guardians for in-depth interview.
The study was guided by Maslow’s Hierarchy of Needs Theory and Ecological Systems Theory, which collectively emphasize that unmet basic needs and interactions within social and environmental systems significantly influence child development and well-being. Findings indicated that child hawking is on the rise, primarily driven by poverty, with most children residing in polygamous families and under the care of parents engaged in petty trading. Children reported exposure to hazards including physical assault, motor accidents, and social
neglect, which adversely affected their academic performance and psychosocial development. Awareness and access to social worker support were minimal, while government policies, though existent, were largely ineffective in addressing child labour.
The study concluded that poverty, inadequate parental support, and low social worker engagement perpetuate child exploitation and hinder child development. Recommendations include implementing targeted poverty
alleviation programs, provision of free education, public sensitization on child labour laws, and strengthened social worker interventions. The study underscores the necessity of coordinated efforts between government, social workers, families, and communities to safeguard children’s rights, promote their welfare, and ensure
holistic development.

authors.name: BALOGUN Michael Adelakun
authors.affiliation: Sociology and Criminology Department, College of Social and Management Sciences, Western Delta University, Oghara, Delta State, Nigeria.

title: INCLUSIVE ENVIRONMENTAL EDUCATION AND STUDENTS’ ENVIRONMENTAL LITERACY: A SYSTEMATIC REVIEW OF PARTICIPATORY AND ACCESSIBLE PEDAGOGICAL STRATEGIES IN NIGERIAN UNIVERSITIES
abstract:
Environmental literacy, defined as the integration of ecological knowledge, environmental attitudes, and pro-environmental behavioural competencies, is increasingly recognised as a critical graduate outcome for addressing Nigeria’s escalating environmental challenges. Despite this importance, the effectiveness of environmental education in Nigerian universities is constrained not only by curricular and resource limitations but also by systemic issues of inclusion that exclude students with disabilities, low literacy backgrounds, and diverse cultural or linguistic orientations from meaningful participation in environmental learning. This systematic review synthesises peer-reviewed and grey literature on inclusive environmental education strategies
and their relationship with environmental literacy outcomes, with specific relevance to Nigerian higher education institutions.
The review followed a structured systematic methodology involving a predefined research question, explicit inclusion and exclusion criteria, multi-database searching, and thematic synthesis of eligible studies. Evidence was drawn from both African and international higher education contexts where inclusive pedagogical approaches were implemented. The synthesis focuses on how accessibility-oriented instruction, participatory and community-based learning, integration of indigenous ecological knowledge, and Universal Design for
Learning principles shape cognitive, affective, and behavioural dimensions of environmental literacy.
Findings indicate that inclusive environmental education approaches consistently enhance environmental literacy outcomes, particularly among students traditionally marginalised in conventional didactic learning environments. The review highlights five key evidence domains: conceptualisation of environmental literacy in African universities, structural barriers to inclusion in Nigerian environmental education, effectiveness of participatory pedagogies, governance and curriculum constraints, and the role of indigenous ecological knowledge in shaping inclusive learning frameworks.
The study concludes that strengthening environmental literacy in Nigerian universities requires a deliberate shift toward inclusive, participatory, and culturally responsive pedagogical systems rather than incremental curriculum adjustments alone.

title: Social Work and Development in a Changing World
abstract: In a world evolving global landscape marked by technology, environmental challenges, and shifting societal norms, social work plays a crucial role in promoting development and addressing social issues. This paper explores the contributions of social work to development, emphasizing community engagement, advocacy for social justice, provision of support services and interdisciplinary collaboration. It is a profession that adapts to promote wellbeing, address inequalities and uphold ethical standards, social work apply key theories like ecological systems, and empowerment theory to understand and address complex client’s needs, navigating ethical dilemmas around confidentiality, self-determinations, and cultural competence. The articles highlight the contributions of social worker as a profession and as a discipline in today’s changing world, emphasizing the profession adaptability to ethical commitment and pursuit of justice, equality and social inclusion to the vulnerable and the oppressed populations. This paper explores the contributions of social work to development emphasizing advocacy for social justice, provision of support services, integrating evidence based practices to guide intervention and to enhance outcome in diverse settings in today’s changing world. A literature review reveals that social work interventions lead to positive outcomes in community development, empowerment and wellbeing. Effective practice requires understanding local context and adapting to changing challenges. As social workers navigate complexities in changing world, the wellbeing of the vulnerable remains crucial. This paper underscores the importance of responsive and adaptable social work practice in contributing to equitable and just development outcomes in today’s changing world as social workers are committed to the live of the oppressed.

authors.name: OJIMS CHIKORDI
authors.affiliation: RIVERS STATE UNIVERSITY, PORT HARCOURT, RIVERS STATE, NIGERIA.

title: Influence of Family support on Mental Health of Senior Secondary School Adolescents in Ibadan South-West, Nigeria.
abstract: Adolescent mental health is an emerging concern in Nigeria, as it negatively impacts academic performance, social relationships, and development, particularly among senior secondary school adolescents. This study examined the influence of family support on the mental health of senior secondary school adolescents in Ibadan South-West Local Government, Oyo State. Quantitative descriptive design was adopted, and 382 students were selected from six schools using multistage sampling. Data were collected with a structured questionnaire incorporating the Depression, Anxiety, and Stress Scale (DASS-10) and the Family Support Scale (FSS). Descriptive and inferential analyses, including linear regression, were conducted. Results indicated that 42.4% of respondents were at risk of poor mental health. Family support was significantly associated with mental health outcomes (R = 0.321, p < 0.001), accounting for 10.3% of the variance. Adolescents with stronger family support reported better mental health outcomes. Based on the findings, it was recommended that strengthening family bonds and integrating social work services into schools and communities are essential for promoting adolescent mental health.

title: MENTAL HEALTH AND MEDICAL SOCIAL WORK INTERVENTION IN RIVERS STATE
abstract:
Mental health conditions, including depression and anxiety disorders, are increasingly recognized as significant public health challenges worldwide. In Nigeria, particularly in Rivers State, the psychosocial and emotional well-being of patients with mental health disorders is often undermined by limited access to professional care, social stigma, and inadequate institutional support. This study examines the roles of medical social workers in the management of mental health conditions, the challenges affecting the effectiveness of their interventions, and the impact of these interventions on patients’ psychosocial well-being and recovery outcomes. The system theory was adopted for theoretical clarity for the study.

The study employed a descriptive survey research design involving a qualitative method, utilizing semi-structured interviews with medical social workers in Rivers State. Thematic analysis was used to analyze the responses. Findings revealed that medical social workers play critical roles in counseling, case management, family engagement, advocacy, and community awareness, all of which enhance emotional stability, social functioning, and reintegration into families and communities. However, their effectiveness is hindered by
resource limitations, workforce shortages, social stigma, economic barriers, and institutional constraints. The study further demonstrated that well-coordinated social work interventions positively influence patients’ psychosocial well-being, adherence to treatment, and recovery outcomes, underscoring the importance of integrating social work services into mental healthcare delivery.

Based on the findings, the study recommends strengthening policy support and funding for mental health services, enhancing the training and capacity of medical social workers, and promoting community awareness to reduce stigma. These measures are essential for improving the quality, accessibility, and sustainability of
mental health care in Rivers State.

title: IMPACT OF SOCIAL WORK PRACTICE ON MALE VICTIMS OF DOMESTIC VIOLENCE IN AHMADU BELLO UNIVERSITY TEACHING HOSPITAL (ABUTH SHIKA) ZARIA, KADUNA STATE
abstract:
Domestic violence against men remains significantly underrecognised in Nigeria, where dominant patriarchal
norms construct masculinity as incompatible with victimhood. This study examines social work practice with
male victims at Ladoke Akintola University Teaching Hospital (LAUTECH), Oyo State, using an instrumental
case study design involving three male outpatients presenting with intimate partner violence-related injuries.
Data were generated through semi-structured interviews, clinical records, and social work case notes, and
analysed using cross-case thematic analysis. Findings indicate a clear institutional imbalance: while male victimisation is medically identified and documented, psychosocial and rehabilitative responses remain limited,
generic, and inconsistently applied. Social work interventions are shaped by gendered service assumptions
that prioritise female victimisation frameworks, resulting in inadequate responsiveness to male victims’ needs.
Socioeconomic class further influences both help-seeking trajectories and participants’ capacity to critically
assess service shortcomings.
Across all cases, there was no evidence of trauma-informed care, structured safety planning, or mental health
screening, despite indications of psychological distress. The study concludes that LAUTECH’s social work
system reflects broader structural invisibility of male domestic violence victims within Nigerian welfare and
health policy frameworks, producing a predominantly medical-forensic response with limited psychosocial
depth. It recommends gender-inclusive training for social workers, the development of male-specific service
pathways, integration of trauma-informed protocols, and national policy reform recognising male victimisation in domestic violence frameworks.

authors.name: Salim Sharhabilu
authors.affiliation: Department of Sociology, Ahmadu Bello University Zaria

title: UNIVERSITY SUSTAINABILITY INITIATIVES AND STUDENT WELL-BEING: A SYSTEMATIC REVIEW OF GREEN SPACE PROVISION, SUSTAINABLE FOOD OPTIONS, AND PHYSICAL AND MENTAL HEALTH OUTCOMES AT THE UNIVERSITY OF NIGERIA, NSUKKA
abstract:
Student well-being, encompassing physical health, mental health, psychological resilience, and overall quality of life, has become a central concern in global higher education. In Nigerian universities, however, student well-being is increasingly compromised by inadequate health infrastructure, overcrowded academic environments, and persistent socioeconomic stressors that shape both academic performance and daily living conditions. Despite this, limited empirical attention has been given to the role of university sustainability initiatives—such as campus green spaces, sustainable food systems, walkable environments, and biophilic infrastructure—in shaping student well-being outcomes. This study therefore presents a systematic review of the relationship between campus sustainability features and student well-being, using the University of Nigeria, Nsukka (UNN) as a focal case study due to its ecological campus design, established green infrastructure, and diverse student population.
The review adopts a protocol-driven systematic methodology, including a predefined research question, multi-database search strategy, structured inclusion and exclusion criteria, two-stage screening process, and critical appraisal using the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool, followed by thematic synthesis. The analysis is guided by an integrated theoretical framework combining the World Health Organization’s holistic model of health, Kaplan and Kaplan’s Attention Restoration Theory, Maslow’s Hierarchy of Needs, and Bronfenbrenner’s Ecological Systems Theory. These frameworks collectively explain how environmental conditions,
nutritional access, physical activity opportunities, and institutional structures interact to influence student physical and psychological well-being in higher education settings.
Findings are synthesised across five thematic domains: the current state of student well-being in Nigerian universities; the ecological and spatial characteristics of the UNN campus environment; evidence linking exposure to green spaces with mental and physical health outcomes; the role of sustainable food provision in shaping nutrition and psychological well-being; and the governance and institutional dimensions of campus sustainability. The review concludes that campus sustainability is a core determinant of student well-being and should be integrated into higher education policy and planning as a foundational component of student welfare
and institutional development.

authors.name: Mina Margaret Ogbanga
authors.affiliation: Department of Social Work, Faculty of Social Sciences Rivers State University, Port Harcourt, Nigeria

title: Èkó Nkẹ́kọ́ and Inclusive Education in Lagos: Leveraging SDG 4 to Address Educational Inequalities and Social Vulnerabilities among Adults in Vulnerable Urban Communities
abstract: The implementation of Èkó Nkẹ́kọ́ (Lagos is Learning) as a strategic initiative that emphasizes inclusive and equitable quality education is the focus of this research. This paper analyzes how the program addresses educational inequalities, especially among adults from vulnerable urban communities such as those affected by marginalization, labor difficulties, and poverty in Lagos, Nigeria. By integrating the principles of social work, Eko Nkẹ́kọ́ provides not only access to education but also psychosocial support, counseling, and advocacy for adults facing barriers to learning. This article explores the social welfare outcomes of facilitating learner engagement, promoting retention, and strengthening community partnerships, thereby mitigating social risks that undermine educational outcomes. Drawing on empirical data, policy analysis, and best practice models, the study identifies successes and challenges in operationalizing inclusive education in Lagos. The findings underscore that initiatives like Èkó Nkẹ́kọ́ can serve as a model for addressing systemic educational inequalities, enhancing learner well-being, and advancing social justice. The article concludes by recommending policy enhancements, capacity building for educators, social workers, and community-driven strategies to sustain inclusive education and achieve SDG 4 in urban Nigerian contexts.
keywords: Keywords: èkó nkẹ́kọ́, inclusive education, educational inequalities, social vulnerability, SDG 4

title: SUSTAINABLE WATER MANAGEMENT AND UNIVERSITY OPERATIONS: A SYSTEMATIC REVIEW OF RAINWATER HARVESTING, WATER-SAVING TECHNOLOGIES, GREYWATER RECYCLING, AND DEMAND MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES AND THEIR EFFECTS ON WATER CONSERVATION OUTCOMES AT THE UNIVERSITY OF IBADAN, NIGERIA
abstract:
Water conservation constitutes an urgent sustainability challenge for Nigerian federal universities amid increasing water scarcity. The University of Ibadan (UI), Nigeria’s oldest university, reflects the cumulative effects of deferred infrastructure maintenance, expanding student population, and the absence of systematic water demand management. This review examines the potential of sustainable water management strategies to produce measurable conservation outcomes at UI.
A systematic review was conducted following a protocol-first approach with a predefined PICO-structured research question, comprehensive multi-database search, explicit inclusion and exclusion criteria, two-stage independent screening, standardised data extraction, critical appraisal using the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool, and thematic synthesis. The theoretical framework integrates political economy of water governance, social-ecological systems resilience, behavioural psychology of conservation, and institutional design for com-
mon-pool resource management.
The review identifies five key intervention categories: rainwater harvesting systems leveraging Ibadan’s ~1,200mm annual rainfall for non-potable use; water-saving technologies such as low-flow fixtures and sensor taps; greywater recycling systems for irrigation and toilet flushing; smart metering for leak detection and consumption monitoring; and demand management strategies combining education, behavioural nudges, usage targets, and conservation pricing. Evidence from international and African contexts suggests these interven-
tions can reduce per capita water consumption by 30–50% within five years when properly implemented and institutionally supported. However, UI currently exhibits unmetered supply systems, deferred maintenance, and no structured conservation programme. Outcomes are therefore highly dependent on infrastructure reliability, governance strength, and institutional commitment.
Sustainable water management at UI is technically feasible and empirically supported but requires a shift from isolated infrastructure provision to embedded institutional practice. Effective outcomes depend on coordinated technical, behavioural, and governance interventions supported by sustained institutional investment and accountability structures.

authors.name: Mezewo Emerinwe Obuzor
authors.affiliation: Department of Social Work Faculty of Social Sciences Rivers State University, Port Harcourt, Nigeria

authors.name: Mina Margaret Ogbanga
authors.affiliation: Department of Social Work Faculty of Social Sciences Rivers State University, Port Harcourt, Nigeria

title: SOCIO-ECONOMIC CHARACTERISTICS OF YOUTH ORGANIZATIONS AND THE FACTORS AFFECTING YOUTH EFFECTIVENESS IN COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT IN RIVERS STATE
abstract:
This research explored the socio-economic traits of youth groups and the aspects influencing their effectiveness in community advancement in Rivers State. A descriptive survey methodology was utilized to systematically collect information from registered youth organizations throughout the state. The total number included
283 youth organizations as recorded by governmental sources. A multistage sampling technique was applied, splitting the study region into three senatorial areas—Rivers East, Rivers West, and Rivers Southeast, from the Local Government Areas selected at random. An equal selection process led to six officials being chosen
from each organization, resulting in an initial sample size of 348 participants, with 247 valid replies (70% response rate). Data collection was carried out via structured questionnaires that underwent validation regarding content and reliability (test-retest coefficient = 0.94). The analysis utilized descriptive statistics and Analysis
of Variance (ANOVA).
Findings indicated that youth organizations are largely dominated by males (73.3%) and primarily consist of
individuals aged 36–42 years, with an average age of 33 years, showing a mature and economically productive
membership. The majority of respondents were single (66.8%), with moderate household sizes and relatively high education levels, as 61.9% held HND/BSc qualifications. Nevertheless, unemployment was prevalent (53.0%), and income levels were low, highlighting economic vulnerability. The duration of membership was relatively extensive, with 61.9% possessing 9–12 years of experience, while funding was mainly derived internally (61.9%), with limited support from external sources. The study additionally pinpointed key limitations impacting effectiveness, such as inadequate funding (4.40), corrupt practices (4.28), poor oversight (4.19), gaps in technical skills (4.21), and illiteracy (3.99).
Regression analyses indicated that socio-economic factors like age, educational background, and years of membership significantly affect the roles of youth (p < 0.05), while ANOVA results showed notable regional variances in constraints (F = 5.245, p = 0.006). The research concludes that while youth organizations play
crucial roles in community development, their effectiveness is hindered by financial, institutional, and structural issues. It is suggested that the government and development partners enhance financial and institutional assistance to youth organizations through grants and capacity-building programs. Moreover, improving training in leadership, technical abilities, and organizational management is essential to boost the overall effectiveness and sustainability of youth-led community advancement initiatives.

authors.name: Dr. Leton De-Great, Kasi Blessing
authors.affiliation: Department of Social Work, Faculty of Social Sciences Rivers State University, Port Harcourt, Nigeria

title: SOCIAL WORKS AND POVERTY REDUCTION IN MBO LOCAL GOVERTMENT AREA, AKWA IBOM STATE,NIGERIA
abstract:
This paper investigated the effect of social works on poverty reduction in Mbo Local Government Area, Akwa Ibom State., inequality, and human development outcomes for the people of Nigeria. The study adopted a survey research design to gather quantitative data on the effect of social work on poverty reduction in Mbo Local Government Area. The total population was estimated at 2150, this consisted of rural household heads who
had directly benefited from or were involved in social work projects within the selected communities.
Taro Yamane’s sample size determination technique was deployed in determining the study’s sample size which was 337. After the return of the questionnaire by the respondents, the data was coded and analyzed using the Statistical Package for Social Science (SPSS) with interpretation made using frequency tables and percentages. The chi-square statistical analysis was used in measuring the hypotheses of the study. From the analysis, the findings revealed that there exist significant relationship between social works and poverty reduction in Mbo Local Government Area especially in the area of vocational training, economic empowerment and social protection.
However, it was concluded among others that Governments, donors, and stakeholders need to ensure adequate and sustained funding for community-based development projects. Reliable financial support is critical to overcoming resource constraints and enabling projects to effectively address poverty reduction goals.
Also that the coordinators of social work initiatives in Mbo Local Government Area should Establish robust management frameworks and transparent monitoring systems that can help address inefficiencies and ensure accountability.

authors.name: VICTOR ABANG
authors.affiliation: Department of Social Work, Faculty of Social Sciences, Rivers State University, Emoha Campus

title: SOCIAL WORK AND COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT: A SYNERGISTIC APPROACH IN RIVERS STATE NIGERIA
abstract:
The author wrote in this paper how social work and community development intersect in the treatment of complex socio-economic and environmental issues in Rivers State, Nigeria. Rivers State is a part of Niger Delta region, which is witnessing colossal issues like oil-related environmental degradation, poverty and social inequality. All these challenges notwithstanding, the integration of social work and community development solutions is a promising future of sustainable development.
In this paper, I would discuss how professional social work practice in conjunction with community development practices could build the capacity of the local population, empower marginalized populations, and bring about social change in the long term. The paper utilizes the case studies of youth empowerment programmes, women cooperative development programmes, and environmental health programmes to demonstrate the effectiveness of the incorporation of social work interventions into community-based programmes.
There are main conclusions that, with the help of such an organic plan, it are possible not only to reduce criminality, as well as to improve economic conditions, but also to enhance social solidarity. However, there are setbacks such as poor professional acknowledgement, lack of finance and institutional issues that inhibit the full potential of such an integrated approach. The paper will conclude by suggesting how this co-operation can be improved in future by policymaking, professional training and mobilizing resources. Finally, the study offers an exemplar on how to combine social work and community development practice in order to promote social justice and sustainable development in the Niger Delta and other parts of the world.

authors.name: Daniel Nkechinyere
authors.affiliation: Department of Social Work, Faculty of Social Sciences University of Port Harcourt, Rivers State University, Port Harcourt, Nigeria

title: INVESTIGATING THE ROLE OF YOUTH ORGANIZATIONS IN PROMOTING HEALTH AND EDUCATIONAL INITIATIVES FOR SUSTAINABLE COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT IN RIVERS STATE
abstract:
This research examined how youth organizations facilitate health and educational efforts aimed at fostering sustainable community progress in Rivers State, Nigeria. A descriptive survey research framework was used to methodically analyze the impact of youth organizations throughout the state. The study sample includes 283
registered youth organizations, from which 23 Local Government Areas using multistage sampling method across the three senatorial districts. The research aimed to survey 348 youth officials, ultimately receiving 247 valid responses, which accounts for a 70% response rate. Data gatherings were carried out with structured
questionnaires that were validated for both face and content, and reliability was confirmed with a test-retest method resulting in a coefficient of 0.94. For data evaluation, descriptive statistics and Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) techniques were applied.
The findings indicated that youth organizations have a crucial influence on health development primarily through awareness initiatives, including campaigns on HIV/AIDS, drug abuse prevention, family nutrition education, and the promotion of treated mosquito nets. Nonetheless, their participation in health infrastructure
and service provision was determined to be limited. In the educational domain, youth organizations significantly contribute through scholarships and the supply of essential learning materials but demonstrate little involvement in infrastructure projects such as building schools or training teachers. The ANOVA analysis
showed notable variations in the roles of youth organizations in health and educational programs across the
three senatorial districts.
The study concludes that youth organizations significantly aid community development, although their contributions are predominantly focused on awareness activities and vary by region. It suggests improved funding, capacity building, and institutional support to enhance their effectiveness in sustainable community development.

authors.name: Dr. Leton De-Great, Kasi Blessing
authors.affiliation: Department of Social Work, Faculty of Social Sciences Rivers State University, Port Harcourt, Nigeria

title: THE ROLE OF PSYCHOSOCIAL EMPOWERMENT STRATEGIES IN PROMOTING RESILIENCE AND WELL BEING AMONG MARGINALIZED URBAN YOUTHS THROUGH SOCIAL WORK PRACTICE IN IBADAN, OYO STATE
abstract:
This study examined the role of psychosocial empowerment strategies in promoting resilience and well-being among marginalized urban youths through social work practice in Ibadan, Oyo State. Urban marginalized
youths face poverty, exclusion, and limited psychosocial support, which harm their mental health and coping abilities. The study explored how social work interventions counseling, mentorship, life skills training, and
psychoeducation can effectively enhance resilience and well-being in this vulnerable group. Using a quantitative design, survey data were collected from 154 youths in selected Ibadan communities. Data analysis
employed ANOVA and multiple regression techniques.
ANOVA showed a statistically significant effect of psychosocial empowerment strategies on resilience and well-being (F(1,136) = 1.712, p = 0.032), confirming the positive impact of empowerment-based interventions. Multiple regression analysis revealed that these strategies jointly predicted resilience and well-being (F(4,149) = 22.88, p < 0.001), with an R2 of 0.381 indicating that 38.1% of the variance in outcomes was due to the four strategies. Among the strategies, counseling (β = 0.278, p < 0.001) and mentorship (β = 0.243, p = 0.003) were the most influential, while life skills training (β = 0.168, p = 0.027) had a moderate effect. Although psychoeducation showed a positive association, it was not statistically significant (p = 0.088). These findings underscore the importance of prioritizing counseling and mentorship in social work programs designed for marginalized youth. By focusing on these strategies, social workers can foster resilience, improve mental well-being, and support positive youth development in urban settings.

authors.name: Temitayo Omolade ADEGBOYEGA
authors.affiliation: Department of Social Work Faculty of Management and Social Sciences Lead City University, Ibadan, Oyo State, Nigeria

authors.name: Hafsat Oyebola BADMUS
authors.affiliation: Department of Social Work Faculty of Management and Social Sciences Lead City University, Ibadan, Oyo State, Nigeria

authors.name: Khadijat Olamiposi BALOGUN
authors.affiliation: Department of Social Work Faculty of Management and Social Sciences Lead City University, Ibadan, Oyo State, Nigeria

title: MARITAL INSTABILITY AND SOCIAL WORK INTERVENTION IN RIVERS STATE
abstract:
This study examines marital instability and social work intervention in Rivers State. It revealed the extent to which marital instability has led to divorce, poor child upbringing, and social maladjustment among families
in Rivers State and examined the role of social work interventions including family therapy, mediation, and family support services in mitigating these effects.
The family system theory by Murray Bowen in the 1950s was adopted. The research adopted a descriptive survey design, targeting 80 married couples and 20 professional social workers in Rivers State. Data were collected using a structured questionnaire and analyzed through simple percentage analysis to determine trends and patterns in respondents’ perceptions. The findings revealed that marital instability has a significant negative impact on families, contributing to increased divorce rates, behavioral and emotional problems among children, poor academic performance, and reduced family cohesion.
Furthermore, the study found that social work interventions have been effective in reducing marital instability, improving spousal communication, strengthening couples’ capacity to manage marital stress, and enhancing overall family stability. Based on these findings, the study recommends the strengthening of family counseling
and therapy programs, the institutionalization of mediation and conflict resolution services, and the expansion of family support services and awareness programs to support marital stability and promote healthy family functioning in Rivers State. The study concludes that social work interventions are essential in mitigating the effects of marital instability and fostering resilient and cohesive family systems.

title: The Security-Sustainability Swap: Military Carbon Debt and Atmospheric Necropolitics in Operation Epic Fury
abstract:
Abstract
In 2026, the global geopolitical landscape is defined by the volatile convergence of kinetic warfare and environmental crisis, most dangerously manifested in the Middle Eastern conflict codenamed Operation Epic Fury. This paper examines atmospheric warfare, a biopolitical and necropolitical project that seeks to enclose, surveil, and destroy life through the weaponization of the environment. By triangulating Biopolitics, Necropolitics, and Neorealism, the study assesses how current regional conflicts exacerbate the military emissions gap and disrupt the 1.3 trillion USD annual climate finance trajectory established at COP30 in Belém. The paper utilizes a methodology that integrates the Comprehensive Climate Impact Quantification (C-CIQ) toolkit with Forensic Architecture and remote sensing forensics. And the findings reveal that military activities account for 5.5% of global emissions. The paper concludes that the securitization of public budgets creates a direct fiscal trade-off that undermines the Paris Agreement, and recommends mandatory emissions reporting alongside a 15% reallocation of defense spending to climate resilience funds.
Keywords: Atmospheric warfare, Biopolitics, Climate diplomacy, Loss and Damage Military emissions.
funding_info: This research received no specific grant from any funding agency in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.
